prompt: Per-site missingness was computed separately for indica and temperate japonica samples at the same set of variant sites. A subset of sites shows high missingness in one subpopulation but low missingness in the other. Interpret this pattern and its consequences for joint analyses.
context:
Differential missingness analysis (sites with >10% missingness in at least one subpopulation):

  Category                                          N_sites    Example_region
  High miss in indica, low in temp_jap              84,210     chr08:24.2 Mb (indica-specific SV)
  High miss in temp_jap, low in indica              12,408     chr04:31.8 Mb (japonica deletion)
  High miss in both                                 142,802    Pericentromeric regions

Missingness rates for Category 1 sites:
  indica mean miss: 0.42, temp_jap mean miss: 0.02
Missingness rates for Category 2 sites:
  indica mean miss: 0.01, temp_jap mean miss: 0.38

Total sites analyzed: 18,412,308
Differentially missing sites: 96,618 (0.52% of total)
correct_answer: Differential missingness between subpopulations is caused by structural variants (SVs) that are polymorphic between but largely fixed within subpopulations. When a large deletion or inversion is present in one subpopulation but absent in the other, reads from the SV-carrying subpopulation cannot map to the reference at those sites, creating systematic missingness. Category 1 (84,210 sites high miss in indica): These likely overlap indica-specific deletions or rearrangements relative to the Nipponbare (temperate japonica) reference. Because the reference IS temperate japonica, indica-specific SVs cause mapping failures in indica but not in temperate japonica. Category 2 (12,408 sites high miss in temperate japonica): These overlap japonica-specific deletions where temperate japonica samples lack the sequence. Despite the reference being temperate japonica, this can occur when the reference individual (Nipponbare) carries a minor allele that is deleted in most other temperate japonica accessions. Consequences for joint analyses: (1) GWAS: Differentially missing sites create spurious associations if missingness is correlated with phenotype and subpopulation. (2) FST: These sites are ascertained only in one subpopulation, biasing FST estimates. (3) PCA/ADMIXTURE: Mean imputation of missing data at these sites pushes the subpopulation with more missing data toward the centroid, creating artifacts. (4) Recommendation: Flag differentially missing sites (missingness difference >20% between subpopulations) and either exclude them or use methods robust to informative missingness.
rubric: Full marks (3): Must identify structural variants as the cause, explain the reference bias mechanism (indica SVs cause indica-specific missingness because reference is japonica), and discuss at least 2 consequences for downstream analyses (GWAS, PCA, FST). Mostly correct (2): Identifies SV cause and reference bias but missing downstream consequences. Partial (1): Notes differential missingness exists but attributes to sequencing depth. Incorrect (0): Claims missingness is random.
notes: Differential missingness from structural variants is a major but under-recognized source of bias in multi-subpopulation analyses.
